 Must visit in winter, for all ages
 Dec 2025
 A must visit in winter. I visited this Park on a wintry sunny day after 4 days of dense fog. The Park was crowded but due to its vast area, it was quite comfortable. The Park has many biodiversity zones as well as Mughal-era structures with waterbodies and a variety of colourful flowers, not to mention birds. I spotted peacock, parrots, eagle and myna. Great for families and even small groups or just go solo and read a book next to the lake. Sunder Nursery also regularly has events, including bird walks, tree walks as well as crafts mela, annual christmas market and weekend bazaar of organic produce and products. Check out their website for more.

 Lovely Gardens in a Busy City
 Feb 2024 • Friends
 The Sunder Nursery is a large park that was once a Mughal era garden and tomb complex. After the end of the British Raj era, the area was left unattended for decades, falling into ruin before restoration finally began in the late 2000s. The restored gardens are amazing. The nursery is filled with flower beds, well tender lawns, plant and there nursery. A surprisingly well tended park considering the state of many Delhi parks. The nursery is also home to many Mughal tombs of important members of the Mughal administration, though not any of the emperors. The most important tomb is that belonging to Mirza Muzaffar Hussain. While Sundarwala Burj, Sundarwala Mahal and Lakkarwala Burj are also worth seeking out.

 Spring Paradise in the heart of Delhi
 Feb 2023
 The place was super crowded.. But I am attribute that to the fact that the weather was conducive to families and groups enjoying the Delhi spring. The place was like a spring paradise, it had everything one needed; Like flowers in all colors, shapes and sizes, birds chirping, clean water bodies with aquatic birds gracing them. It was clean (for a change compared to other parks in Delhi). And it had security personnel monitoring the place. (BIG PLUS) There is a play area for kids with many options ; and a tree house. The nursery is garnished with historical monuments that stand a witness to the past and cater to a future generation. My only grudge... is that the food options are limited; and highly overpriced. It would be best to carry ones own food. but that kind of dampens the "keep it light and easy" spirit. All is all a good place to invest over a weekend.
 DO note that they have entry fee of rs 50 per adult, rs 25 for senior citizens and kids, and Rs 100 for car parking. Those expenses I found worthwhile due to the facilities provided.

 It's a great place for picnic , one is allowed to carry eatables and picnic baskets inside ,can spread a sheet and sit in the sprawling lawns .

 Today was Christmas and they have an annual Christmas Market - The Sorbet Soiree having stalls of clothes ,accessories , food ,home decor etc .

 Nice but a huge distance from Gurgaon!
 Dec 2022
 The directions in the park has to be more prominent and clear and has to be placed more frequently.
 
 The food stalls are a disappointment after a long, long walk. The small food stalls have mostly chips, biscuits, tea and coffee, and some patties. Only few food vans have sandwiches, which are located at another place.
 
 The full-fledged restaurant by Fab India has a mix of Indian, Thai, italian cuisine. The Ladakhi soup noodles we ordered was of good quantity, light and mildly spicy. However, it is a very busy spot and might take a long time to receive the food. Seating arrangement is small, so there is a rush during lunch hours.
 
 Good restroom facilities. Nice picnic spots throughout the park. It has an amphitheater, which can be booked in advance for holding programmes.
 
 Overall, a nice place to spend with family and friends on a winter afternoon.
 
 Please note: The Drive Way is different from the Walk Way. The Drive Way will lead to the Fab India restaurant. It is a longer distance. The Walk Way is a shorter route to the restaurant. Please do not use the Drive Way if you are walking to the restaurant. You will end up walking miles through an isolated paved forest road, which may make you feel uncanny, though it is safe.

 Sundar Nursery restored by the Aga Khan Trust for Culture has created a beautiful lush green public space in Delhi. Such is the level of detailing that even the toilets at a far corner have been aesthetically been designed to blend in with the surroundings.
 
 But the highlight of this botanical park are its trees, plants & birds. The manicure lawns have varieties of flowers which attract insects like butterflies & the bees. These in turn attract the birds like pigeons, hawks, peacocks, etc. It's a delight to view the birds & the bees at such close proximity.
 
 Go here if you want to enjoy the sunset, beat the heat, love to explore the heritage, gaze at the birds & take in some really fresh air.

keith h

 Magnificent Creation of Tranquility & Beauty.
 Feb 2019 • Solo
 A visit to Sunder Nursery is a must & is a prime antidote to flogging around Delhi's many attraction's, no matter how interesting they are.
 The gardens here have only recently opened to the public but the actual nursery still functions & covers 20 acres. The nursery was created by the British in the Colonial era in order to grow plants for the city.
 Actually, the area was originally a Mughal Garden of the 16c known as Azim Bagh.
 Now, in addition to the Sunder Nursery there are 90 acres of marvellous gardens with formal flower beds cut into well maintained lawns. European visitors will instantly recognise beds of scarlet Salvia, Antirrhinums, Cineraria ( pot plant in colder climes ), Pansies, Sweet William & Mesembryanthemums.
 A watercourse runs straight from the Sunder burj towards the site of the old Azim Ganj Serai, the water punctuated by stylised stone Lotus flowers which emit a trickle of water.
 The garden at the end boundary where the ruins of the Serai can be seen are well furnished with colourful flower beds & more water features.
 Throughout the gardens there are Mughal period mausoleum's & pavilions, most lovingly renovated by craftspeople. There is a large lake with fountains which winds, snake - like as it becomes narrower & turns into a stone flanked channel before running into a natural wild area & opening into large pools.
 Apparently there are plans to extend the whole area to cover some 900 acres. Already the gardens are attracting many birds & butterflies, some rare to such a crowded city.
 Humayun's Tomb is adjacent to Sunder Nursery & at the Azim Ganj end the boundary is shared with Delhi Zoological Gardens, so creating a contiguous area of greenery.
 Like Humayun's Tomb, the Sunder Nursery lies on the route of The Grand Trunk Rd.
 An unusual feature of the garden are 2 Bonsai Houses which retain many specimens from a private collection.
 There is also an amphitheatre for hosting various entertainments.
 Being situated next to Humayun's Tomb, the gardens are easy to find.
 If you want to come by metro, the nearest station is JLN Stadium. From there you can walk in 20 mins if you don't mind dodging over a couple of wide roads or take a 5 min tuk- tuk ride.

 3- the central axis: the symmetry and the elegance of the lines with water flowing quietly from one square pool into another is beautiful to see

 5- the design on the ceiling of the Sundar Burj and the walls of the Bateshewala complex are intricate and boldly displayed: a confident, talented artist drew this!

Madhulika L
 Noida, India8,181 contributions

 Very beautiful indeed
 Mar 2018
 Sunder Nursery is a government-owned plant nursery situated right next door to Humayun’s Tomb. The interesting thing about Sunder Nursery is that while it is obviously a place for horticultural pursuits, it’s also home to several medieval monuments: tombs, pavilions, and so on. Some years back, thanks to the Aga Khan Trust (which has funded and spearheaded several heritage conservation projects in the vicinity, including that of Humayun’s Tomb, the Nizamuddin Dargah and other monuments in Nizamuddin), a project was initiated to make Sunder Nursery a major park, like the city parks so common in the West.
 
 … Except that this park was modelled not on the parks of the West, but the Mughal gardens and, interestingly enough, Persian carpets. Major landscaping was done, hundreds of trees were planted, the medieval monuments in the complex were all restored.
 
 The revamped Sunder Nursery was inaugurated very recently, so I decided to take my toddler along to see it one day while Spring had not yet given way to summer. There is car parking inside, but the day we visited, high security was in place because the Prime Minister was due to visit the next day; fortunately, the parking at Humayun’s Tomb isn’t too far.
 
 We entered through the main gate and into the formal garden, which is like a classic ‘charbagh’, with shallow channels of water (not running yet, but I suppose they will begin soon), carved marble fountains, neat stone benches, and lots of trees and flowers. Right in front, facing the main gate of the nursery, is the small tomb known as Sunderwala Burj. This one looks neat but none too exciting on the outside. Don’t let that out you off: do, do go inside, because the interior consists of some of the most exquisite incised plaster work to be seen in Delhi: it’s a riot of stars and floral patterns, not one inch of the walls or ceiling left bare.
 
 Since we had limited time and it was already pretty hot (plus my daughter wanted to spend more time watching the jet of water in one of the pools), we entered only one other monument, the Sunderwala Mahal. This lies off to the right of Sunderwala Burj and is a single-storeyed covered pavilion, with a series of chambers leading one into the other around a central hall. The decoration here is elegantly minimalistic: niches, some netted vaulted, and that’s it.
 
 We also walked around an interesting high gate-like structure and had a look at the tomb called Lakkadwala Burj from afar. The rest of the time we spent walking around the gardens, admiring the trees (there are over 300 species here in the arboretum), the flowers in the parterres, and the general tranquility and uncrowdedness of it all.
 
 This isn’t completely done yet (for instance, there are still unfinished areas where artificial water bodies are yet to be filled). But, despite that, worth visiting.
